Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic, but it worked for me. I'd typically carve out chunks of time around my other commitments - I'm involved in extracurriculars, so I had to be strategic. I relied heavily on reading and writing things out, so I'd print materials or copy notes into my own documents. I probably studied around 21 hours a week, but it wasn't always at the same times or in the same way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying a few hours each evening. Other weeks, especially when I had events or other responsibilities, I'd have to cram everything into a couple of intensive sessions. Deadlines were the main driver of when I'd really buckle down.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check what was due next or to access specific readings. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something and that would prompt me to go look. I also logged in quite a bit to watch lecture videos or review materials before working on assignments. It wasn't always purposeful browsing - sometimes I just wanted to make sure I wasn't missing anything important.
Q4: Yeah, there was a period mid-semester where logging in became pretty automatic for me. I'd check it most mornings with my coffee, just scanning for updates or new materials. It happened because I realized I'd missed a few things early on due to my attendance issues, so I made the LMS my safety net. It became a habit born out of necessity rather than enthusiasm.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were gold for me - that's how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and lecture notes and really work through them thoroughly. The videos were also helpful, I watched quite a lot of them, especially when I'd missed classes. Being able to pause and rewind while taking notes made a huge difference for my understanding.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums - I think I only posted once the whole semester. It just didn't feel like my thing; I'm more of an independent learner and I found the discussions a bit surface-level. I also skipped some of the optional supplementary materials when I was pressed for time. If something wasn't directly tied to an assessment, it often got deprioritized.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I'm pretty pragmatic - if I could see the payoff, I'd invest the effort. The videos felt worthwhile because they filled gaps from missed lectures, and the readings were essential for my assignments.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When things were calm, I'd explore them, but when stress was medium-high and I had deadlines looming, they were the first things to go. Required activities had this weight to them - I knew they mattered for my grade, so they got prioritized even when I was tired or unmotivated.
Q9: Absolutely. A huge amount of my studying happened offline with downloaded files and my own handwritten notes. I'd print readings, annotate them, and create summary documents. I also used textbooks from the library and sometimes external articles I found through Google Scholar. My read-write learning style means I need to physically work with text, not just scroll through it online.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through printed materials at my desk or in the library with no LMS activity showing at all. I'd download a week's worth of content in one session, then study from those files for days. My exam prep especially was almost entirely offline - I made extensive handwritten notes and practiced writing out answers by hand.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access something once to download it, then never open it on the LMS again even though I might study from that document for weeks. It was more efficient for me to have everything in folders on my laptop and work from there. The LMS was really just a delivery system for materials, not where my actual learning happened.
Q12: My attendance was pretty poor - around 26% - and there were definitely periods where I struggled to engage. Some of it was competing responsibilities with my extracurriculars, and some was just feeling overwhelmed. When stress built up, I'd pull back from everything briefly. The CBT session I had actually helped me work through some of those harder moments and build better coping strategies.
Q13: Definitely. When I was juggling multiple commitments, my LMS usage would drop even though my actual studying might not. Fatigue was a real factor - sometimes I'd be too tired to engage meaningfully with online content but could still read printed notes. Confusion sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand.
Q14: There were times I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered completely elsewhere. I'd technically be watching, but nothing was sinking in. Sometimes I'd scroll through readings without really processing them, especially late at night. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even knowing they were important - my completion rate wasn't great at around 54%. Sometimes it was because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. Other times, my motivation was just low and I'd convince myself I'd do it tomorrow. The irony is that when I did complete assignments, I did well on them.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd have this surge of LMS activity in the days before something was due - downloading materials, rewatching videos, checking requirements. Without deadlines, I'd drift. They created urgency that cut through my lower motivation levels. My best work happened under that pressure, even if it wasn't the healthiest approach.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was positive - seeing good marks on assignments I'd worked hard on made me want to maintain that standard. When I got my exam score back and it was strong, it validated that my approach was working despite the messy attendance. Constructive feedback helped me adjust my focus for subsequent work.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my schedule and catch up via videos when I missed classes. Bad because without rigid structure, I could let things slide. The clear assessment deadlines were the scaffolding that kept me on track when my own motivation wasn't enough.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for someone with my learning style. The video content being available asynchronously was crucial given my attendance issues. What made it harder was when things weren't clearly organized or when I couldn't find what I needed quickly - I'd lose momentum and sometimes give up for that session.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about the material. They might be searching, rewatching, checking and rechecking because they're not confident. Or they could be struggling to understand what's actually required for assignments. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of confusion rather than engagement.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS activity probably looked moderate, but I performed well because so much of my learning happened offline. I'd download materials efficiently and then disappear to study independently. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't engaged, but my assignment scores and exam results tell a different story.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone logs in, what they click on, how long videos play. But they can't show understanding or effort that happens away from the screen. They miss printed materials being studied, handwritten notes being made, concepts being discussed with friends. They capture digital footprints, not learning itself.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and wanting to understand it, not just get through it. It's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when I'm curious to go deeper. It's not about how much time I log online - it's about whether the content is actually occupying space in my mind and connecting to other things I know.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew in managing my stress and building resilience this semester. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed readings at coffee shops, or the study sessions with peers that happened face-to-face. It definitely wouldn't show that despite my poor attendance and incomplete assignments, I was deeply committed to actually learning the material that mattered for exams.
